name: Larry Coryell
abstract: Larry Coryell (born Lorenz Albert Van DeLinder III; April 2, 1943 – February 19, 2017) was an American jazz guitarist, widely considered the "godfather of fusion". Alongside Gábor Szabó, he was a pioneer in melding jazz, country and rock music.

about_quotes.speaker: Bley Carla
about_quotes.evidence: That was the one with Bob Moses, Larry Coryell, Steve Swallow and Gary. And we practiced it, we rehearsed it out in California I remember, and recorded it in New York.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bley Carla — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: I put him on the band... Larry and Arnie came on the band and that’s how we did The Dealer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Burton
about_quotes.evidence: A few years later, I had my own band, and started with Larry Coryell playing guitar
about_quotes.doc_title: Gary Burton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Larry Coryell in a hypothetical comparison about pay rates; the interviewee (Jimmy Cobb) does not respond to or acknowledge the mention of Larry Coryell.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: They wanted me to fire Eric Dolphy. They wanted me to fire Charles Lloyd. Larry Coryell. ... These guys turned out to be young geniuses.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 30, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: It was originally supposed to be for Larry Coryell, Richard Davis, and a trio. You know, and Larry didn’t show up.
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
